speaker: interviewer
text: It's-- you know, I mean-- if you want to you don't, have to. You know, we can get more tapes and stuff. There is-- I've done interviews where they went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, but I-- well, let's try to get it onto this one because we are so close to the end. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutively [? possible. ?] So where do you know Joyce?

speaker: interviewer
text: Joy, we studied torah together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a new capitalist kind of thing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six with Leon Wendel. Tape six on April 24, 1995. You were talking about finding out that your brother had survived after the war by reading the list of names in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I-- when I-- I-- every day I went to the committee to look at the lists. And one day, everybody who came to Lodz came to the committee. And one day I walked down, and I see my future wife. I met her on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know her before the war? Yeah. Oh yeah, she's a cousin. How is she related to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother's-- my mother and her father were first cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is [? Faiga ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And I say, [? Fela, ?] what-- you're here. Do you have anybody? Do you know somebody? She said, no, she just came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took her up to us, to that apartment we had there, and there was a woman there, too, in their apartment. And she stayed with us. And then maybe not much longer, because we were no more in Lodz than maybe three weeks. So not much longer my brother came to Lodz and he said, you're not gonna stay here. There's nothing here. There's nobody here, and you don't have-- you know. So we created a group, and we went back to Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: To go back to Munich, we went down to the Polish-- it was called a-- a resettlement office. I don't remember exactly how it was pronounced in Polish, and they gave me a piece of paper on which they wrote down that I'm going to look for relatives, that I have some. I heard there is some relatives living in Munich. And each one us got a piece of paper like that, and with that we went on the train, and we went-- we took the international train [INAUDIBLE] Prague. And when we came to the check point, we showed them that paper, you know, and-- and there were Jewish soldiers in the Polish army, and the trains-- and the trains-- there was soldiers with each train, because the trains were not secure. They had problems with the-- with the Polish fighting against the government. So we crossed the border with no problem. We didn't pay nothing, and we came to Prague. And in Prague, there was a Jewish committee, and went down to that Jewish committee every day, and they handed out free food and things like that. And we stayed there overnight, and then we took the next train to the next border, to the German border. And I-- if I remember right, we crossed it by foot, but I'm not sure. The train went over the border. I don't think so. I think we had to get off the train and we just went to the next town-- next small town, which was already inside Germany, and we took a train over there into Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your future wife amongst the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My wife was with us, and my-- my-- Manny's wife-- Manny's future wife was with us, and some more people were with us. We were about 10 people, like I say. And in Munich, we-- we stayed-- we had a couple places. My brother was there already before, so he moved into one-- one room with a couple, and then we got a place with another family-- just one room, and we stayed in that room. And then when they-- they made what they called a-- a camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a displaced persons camp, which was right across from where we lived. We happened to live there. Right across the street they made this camp, so we took an apartment-- not an  apartment, a whole house, a little house in that camp. And we stayed in that camp. This was Freimann-- Neu Freimann it was called-- New Freimann. And we stayed in that camp till-- till were sent-- till we went over to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I got married-- in-- in-- in the meantime, my wife went to Austria. She found her brother. So she stayed there, and then they both came back, the brother and her, to stay with us in Munich. And we got married in 1947-- in-- in October '47. And my brother married a year before, and his wife had some relatives in the United States, so they sent her some papers, and she did get to the United States before me. A-- they came right away to Los Angeles. They did not send any paper for me. There were no relatives of mine, so I went with the general registration with the Joint Distribution Committee until I got the papers. It was already 1950. In the meantime, I already had a two-year old son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son's name is Isidor, Isidor Wendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom is he named?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is named after my wife-- his name is Isidor Kalman Wendel. He is named after my father and after my wife's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was born in 4th of July 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in the States on the 30th of October in 1950 on the I remember it perfectly, and-- and I came to New York. And I stayed in New York maybe three weeks or four weeks. And since my brother was already here--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles-- I went on my own to Los Angeles. The committee did not want to send me over here. So I came over here with the Greyhound bus. It took us four nights and three days with a small child. And when we came down here, I went down to the committee, and they said they want nothing to do with me. I don't know. You want this on the tape or not. They said they're gonna give me a ticket to go back. I said, no thank you. And we stayed here-- you want to go into my--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to know who-- did you have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we have one daughter, Evelyn, who was born in Los Angeles on the 17th of-- April 17, 19-- could you stop it for a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: '52, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm not so sure about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have-- have your children gotten married in the ensuing years?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- and my son got married in-- in 1985.

speaker: interviewer
text: To who?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 12th of October 1985 to my daughter-in-law, Sylvia [INAUDIBLE]. [? Weisse ?], Sylvia [? Weisse ?], whose parents are also refugees, but not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not from concentration camp. They survived another way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your son and daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son and daughter-in-law have one son, Nathan, who is say six and a half years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is your daughter married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter got married two years ago on February 9th-- 7th. I have it written down. I forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1993, and they have no children yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to whom is she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she is married to [? Jeffrey ?] Tipton, and he is American.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that 50 years have passed since you went through the experiences that you went through and you've just spent this time telling us this story of what your experiences were, for the people who will be seeing this tape in the future-- your grandson, and other grandchildren, and others, is there any final message that you would like to leave with them? Well, what can I say? I mean, you learn a lot from life, and you learn nothing. Things never repeat themselves exactly the same way. You-- all I can say is don't always believe what you see. Don't always think what you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned to distrust authority. I learned to distrust the-- the [? rules ?] of figures, the [? rules ?] of things. Life is not a picnic, and it's very serious at all times. And all you ever have is your close family, and believe in that only, because they are the ones when you need to support who will or can give it to you. All the rest is when chips are down, when it gets really, really hard, everybody looks out for himself. People did sell out-- or not sell out. They pushed other people in front of them so they will be left behind. And that was a common thing during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, you have some pictures of your family now to show us, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have quite a few pictures of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much. We'll look at them now. OK. We always say that just so that-- because we turn off the camera and reset it up, but we say that just to sort of shift the focus, but thank you. That was an extraordinary story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could give you a lot of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. I know. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Kalman Wendel. It was named-- it was taken in Lodz, Poland approximately in-- between '16 or '18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is not a uniform. No, this is just a winter coat, or a short winter coat like a-- Balkan people used to wear. It's not a dressing coat. It's an under coat. And the cap is a warm cap.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Esther Chana. And this picture was taken around the same approximate time, like between 1916 or '18 in Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is a picture of my oldest brother, Leib, which I found after the war in the ruins of our house, and that's why the picture is-- is so damaged. Of course, the other pictures are from the same time, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken either in '38 or '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother and my-- and his future wife. My brother, Hershel, and his future wife's name is [INAUDIBLE]. And this was taken in 1938 in Lodz, and they were sent to Chelmno, from where nobody ever came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1938, and it shows me, Leib-- Leon Wendel my mother, Esther Chana Wendel, and my sister, Alta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Lodz in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Mendel, who was a year and a half older than me-- than Leibel-- than Leon, and it was taken in Lodz in 1938 in the front of city hall. This picture was taken in the outskirts of Lodz on-- on-- in the country, not-- not far from Lodz. On the top is Leon Wendel. In the middle holding up is my wife's uncle, [? Arnold ?] [INAUDIBLE]. On the right hand side is my brother Mendel Wendel, and on the left hand side is my brother Manny, or [? Manasha ?], Wendel. It was taken around 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And Manny is your brother who also survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Manny is my brother who also survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Lodz, Poland on the cemetery around 1936 or '37. And it shows the unveiling of my grandmother, [? Shifra's-- ?] [? Shifra ?] Wendel's headstone.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the left-- from the left going up is the youngest uncle, [? Itzik. ?] Next is-- [? Itzik ?] Wendel. Next is my uncle-- what's his name? Next is my-- I don't [? know. ?] [? Moishe. ?] Next is my uncle, Moishe [INAUDIBLE]. Next is [? Moishe ?] [INAUDIBLE] wife, and on top, is my father, Kalman Wendel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going over, across is my oldest uncle, [? Schlomo. ?] Next is my uncle, [? Chaim, ?] and the youngest-- next to the youngest, [INAUDIBLE]. And the other woman is [? Sheva. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are your father's brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: And these are all my father's brothers, sisters, except for [? Moishe ?] [INAUDIBLE], who was in the middle on the left hand side. He's a brother-in-law married to-- what was her name? Could you stop it?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? Moishe ?] [INAUDIBLE] wife is [? Sibyl ?] [? Wendel, ?] my father's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in the summer of 1940 inside the ghetto, and starting from the left on top is my sister Alta Wendel, me, Leon Wendel, my sister-in-law-- at that time-- at that time she was already my sister-in-law, [INAUDIBLE] Wendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, Hershel Wendel, and going back to the bottom, my father, Kalman Wendel, my mother, Esther Chana Wendel, and my brother, Leibel Wendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is after your brother Leibel had been in the army, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his nose had been injured.

speaker: interviewee
text: His nose had been injured, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because that's notable in the photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead. Do it again. Try-- try to put it in the same place that you're picking it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put it down so you'll know where it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and I'll look at that now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You want me to pick it up?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And could you tell her about the ring?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the ring which my mother gave me when I was sent out to the camp and which me and my wife got married. My son and daughter-in-law, and my son-in-law, and my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have all been married?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell the name? My son Isidor, and Isidor to [? Sylvia ?] Wendel and my son-in-law, [? Jeffrey ?] Tipton to Evelyn Wendel. And Leon and Frances Wendel. [INAUDIBLE] the pictures from the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, here we go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Should I say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture, Leon and Frances, or Leon and [? Faiga ?] Wendel in October 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 30th of October 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a borrowed dress. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the wedding picture of my son, Isidor Wendel, to his wife [? Sylvia ?] [? Weisse ?] Wendel. In 19-- in 1985, October 12, 1985, in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her original name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sylvia [? Weisse. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Next. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandson, Nathan Wendel, born in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- the 29th of September, 1980--

speaker: interviewee
text: '88. 1988 in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter's wedding, Evelyn Wendel, to [? Jeffrey ?] Tipton on-- on February 7, 1993 in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.
